title: Injuries and omissions: Who is not going to the 2026 FIFA World Cup?
original_text:
Injuries and omissions: Who is not going to the 2026 FIFA World Cup?
by: Juan Cisneros
Posted: Jun 6, 2026 / 12:01 PM EDT
Updated: Jun 6, 2026 / 12:01 PM EDT
(NEXSTAR) — Every four years, national team managers are tasked with putting together a roster to compete at the highest level at the FIFA World Cup. In 2026, 26 players from each nation will travel to Canada, Mexico and the United States to represent their countries, but with a limited amount of roster spots, tough decisions have to be made, leaving some big names missing from the tournament.
For some, injuries played a role in being left off a World Cup roster, including some that play at some of the biggest clubs in the world.
CountryInjured Players
Austria Christoph Baumgartner (RB Leipzig)
Brazil Estêvão (Chelsea), Rodrygo (Real Madrid), Éder Militão (Real Madrid)
Canada Marcelo Florez (Tigres)
Ecuador Leonardo Campana (New England Revolution)
England Jack Grealish (Everton)
France Hugo Ekitike (Liverpool)
Germany Serge Gnabry (Bayern Munich)
Ghana Mohammed Kudus (Tottenham)
Japan Takumi Minamino (Monaco), Kaoru Mitoma (Brighton)
Mexico Luis Malagón (América), Marcel Ruiz (Toluca)
Netherlands Xavi Simons (Tottenham), Matthijs de Ligt (Manchester United)
Scotland Billy Gilmour (Napoli)
Spain Fermín López (FC Barcelona)
Sweden Emil Holm (Juventus), Dejan Kulusevski (Tottenham)
United States Johnny Cardoso (Atletico Madrid), Patrick Agyemang (Derby County), Cameron Carter-Vickers (Celtic)
And while injuries are part of the game, some players were simply left off their respective rosters based on a coach’s decision.
It’s basically a tradition for fans and analysts to disagree with final roster decisions, but some big time names from championship contenders will be watching the World Cup from home this summer.
England
England’s roster is stacked and making the cut is difficult. But players like Cole Palmer, Phil Foden, Harry Maguire, and Trent Alexander-Arnold were left off by manager Thomas Tuchel.
The 24-year-old Palmer is a starter for Chelsea in the English Premiere League, but Tuchel said that based on his performance for his club this season was not up-to-par with his previous seasons. Palmer also dealt with nagging injuries to his groin, toe, and thigh that required day-to-day monitoring.
Veteran defender Harry Maguire featured for England in their March friendlies, but failed to make a big enough impact to be selected. Similarly, Alexander-Arnold failed to make an impact in his first season with Real Madrid, thus was left of the roster.
France
Another favorite to lift the trophy, France, has an envious amount of depth and player pool to choose from. However, Real Madrid midfielder Eduardo Camavinga missed the cut for the tournament. While Camavinga has been making a name for himself since making his first start for the national team at 18-years-old, his 2025-26 campaign in La Liga was not the brightest, leaving him off the final roster.
Spain
Spain is also a top contender for the ultimate prize. A mix of youth and veteran presence across the roster seemed like a perfect fit for 21-year-old Real Madrid defender Dean Huijsen. But head coach Luis de la Fuente opted to not take Huijsen, who featured and impressed for the Spaniards in the Nations League in 2025.
Argentina
Franco Mastantuono made the jump to Real Madrid on his 18th birthday in 2025 after a stint with River Plate in top-flight Argentine soccer. The young winger made 23 appearances for the Spanish giant in his first season, finding the back of the net once.
While always on the bubble to make the World Cup roster, Mastantuono is the youngest player to debut for the three-time World Champions and has shown promise to develop into a featured element in the attack. However, the depth of attacking options for the defending champions ultimately left the Argentine off Lionel Scaloni’s list.
